3 personas$98,922

4 personas$104,950

Por cada persona adicional arriba de 4, agregue aproximadamente $11,100. Cifras vigentes abril 2026.

Exenciones de Wisconsin

Los declarantes pueden elegir entre exenciones estatales o federales.

Investigue las exenciones de hogar, vehiculo, propiedad personal, comodin y jubilacion de Wisconsin antes de presentar. Sus exenciones determinan que propiedad puede conservar.

Ver exenciones de bancarrota de Wisconsin en detalle

Preguntas Frecuentes

Como me declaro en bancarrota en Wisconsin?

Para declararse en bancarrota en Wisconsin: (1) complete asesoria de credito aprobada por el DOJ, (2) reuna documentacion de ingresos de 6 meses, (3) complete los formularios oficiales, (4) presente ante el tribunal correcto, y (5) pague la cuota ($338 Cap. 7, $313 Cap. 13).

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Wisconsin?

Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $65,080 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $104,950. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba de medios.

Cuanto cuesta declararse en bancarrota en Wisconsin?

La cuota del tribunal para Capitulo 7 es $338 y Capitulo 13 es $313. Asesoria de credito: $15-$50. Educacion del deudor: $15-$50. Honorarios de abogado: $1,200-$3,500 (Cap. 7), $3,000-$5,500 (Cap. 13). Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud de bancarrota. Es un derecho federal.

Bancarrota en Estados Cercanos

Minnesota · Míchigan · Illinois · Iowa

## Como Declararse en Bancarrota en Wyoming - Guia Paso a Paso [2026] - How to File
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states-es/wyoming.html

Como Declararse en Bancarrota | Todos los Estados | English | Calculadora | Screener

Inicio›Estados (Espanol)›Wyoming

Como Declararse en Bancarrota en Wyoming

Guia paso a paso: proceso de presentacion, 1 distrito, cuotas, limites de la prueba de medios, asesoria de credito y que esperar en Wyoming.

Nota importante para inmigrantes: La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden declararse en bancarrota. Es un derecho federal disponible para todos los residentes de Estados Unidos.

Importante: Esta pagina proporciona informacion educativa sobre como declararse en bancarrota en Wyoming. No constituye asesoramiento legal. Las cifras de ingresos medianos son aproximadas (Censo/DOJ, vigentes abril 2026). Verifique los montos actuales en la pagina del DOJ.

1

Distritos de Bancarrota

$67,360

Mediana (1 Persona)

$338

Cuota Cap. 7

$313

Cuota Cap. 13

Pasos Para Declararse en Bancarrota en Wyoming

Paso 1: Asesoria de Credito

Debe completar un curso de asesoria de credito de un proveedor aprobado por el DOJ dentro de los 180 dias anteriores a la presentacion. El costo es generalmente $15-$50. Muchos proveedores ofrecen cursos en espanol.

Paso 2: Determine Su Capitulo

El Capitulo 7 elimina la mayoria de las deudas no garantizadas en 3-4 meses. El Capitulo 13 reestructura las deudas en un plan de pago de 3-5 annos. Compare Capitulo 7 vs Capitulo 13.

Paso 3: Reuna Documentacion

Documentacion de ingresos de los ultimos 6 meses calendario completos

Declaraciones de impuestos de los ultimos 2 annos

Lista de todas las deudas, activos, ingresos y gastos

Estados de cuenta bancarios recientes

Paso 4: Complete los Formularios

Los formularios oficiales de bancarrota incluyen la peticion, declaraciones y anexos. Puede obtenerlos gratis en uscourts.gov.

Paso 5: Presente Ante el Tribunal

Wyoming tiene 1 distrito federal de bancarrota. Presente en el distrito donde ha vivido la mayor parte de los ultimos 180 dias.

Paso 6: Pague las Cuotas

Capitulo 7: $338. Capitulo 13: $313. Puede solicitar pago en cuotas o exencion de la cuota si sus ingresos estan por debajo del 150% de la linea de pobreza.

Despues de Presentar

La suspension automatica entra en efecto inmediatamente, deteniendo cobros, embargos y demandas.

Asista a la reunion 341 de acreedores (generalmente 30-45 dias despues de presentar).

Complete el curso de educacion del deudor (requerido antes de la descarga).

Reciba su descarga (Capitulo 7: ~60-90 dias despues de la reunion 341; Capitulo 13: despues de completar el plan de 3-5 annos).

Wyoming requiere el uso de exenciones estatales solamente.

Investigue las exenciones de hogar, vehiculo, propiedad personal, comodin y jubilacion de Wyoming antes de presentar. Sus exenciones determinan que propiedad puede conservar.

Ver exenciones de bancarrota de Wyoming en detalle

Preguntas Frecuentes

Como me declaro en bancarrota en Wyoming?

Para declararse en bancarrota en Wyoming: (1) complete asesoria de credito aprobada por el DOJ, (2) reuna documentacion de ingresos de 6 meses, (3) complete los formularios oficiales, (4) presente ante el tribunal correcto, y (5) pague la cuota ($338 Cap. 7, $313 Cap. 13).

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Wyoming?

Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $67,360 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $107,280. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba de medios.

Cuanto cuesta declararse en bancarrota en Wyoming?

La cuota del tribunal para Capitulo 7 es $338 y Capitulo 13 es $313. Asesoria de credito: $15-$50. Educacion del deudor: $15-$50. Honorarios de abogado: $1,200-$3,500 (Cap. 7), $3,000-$5,500 (Cap. 13). Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud de bancarrota. Es un derecho federal.

## How to File Bankruptcy in Alabama - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/alabama.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Alabama

How to File Bankruptcy in Alabama

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 3 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Alabama.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Alabama. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$56,050

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Alabama

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Alabama bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Alabama Bankruptcy Districts

Alabama has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of Alabama

Middle District of Alabama

Southern District of Alabama

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Alabama

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Alabama Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$56,050$4,670

2 persons$72,865$6,072

3 persons$85,196$7,099

4 persons$99,208$8,267

5 persons$110,308$9,192

6 persons$121,408$10,117

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Alabama Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Alabama requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Alabama's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Alabama bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Alabama?

To file bankruptcy in Alabama, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Alabama bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Alabama?

The Chapter 7 means test in Alabama compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $56,050 per year. For a family of four, approximately $99,208. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Alabama?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Alabama. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Alabama?

Alabama has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Mississippi · Tennessee · Georgia · Florida

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project - free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

341 Meeting ·

## How to File Bankruptcy in Alaska - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/alaska.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Alaska

How to File Bankruptcy in Alaska

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Alaska.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Alaska. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$73,866

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Alaska

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Alaska bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Alaska Bankruptcy Districts

Alaska has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Alaska

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Alaska

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Alaska Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,866$6,155

2 persons$96,025$8,002

3 persons$112,276$9,356

4 persons$130,742$10,895

5 persons$141,842$11,820

6 persons$152,942$12,745

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Alaska Exemptions

Federal or State

Alaska allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Alaska's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Alaska bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Alaska?

To file bankruptcy in Alaska, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Alaska bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Alaska?

The Chapter 7 means test in Alaska compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $73,866 per year. For a family of four, approximately $130,742. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Alaska?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Alaska. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Alaska?

Alaska has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Washington · Hawaii

## How to File Bankruptcy in Arizona - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/arizona.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Arizona

How to File Bankruptcy in Arizona

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Arizona.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Arizona. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$65,014

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Arizona

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Arizona bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Arizona Bankruptcy Districts

Arizona has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Arizona

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Arizona

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Arizona Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$65,014$5,417

2 persons$84,518$7,043

3 persons$98,821$8,235

4 persons$115,074$9,589

5 persons$126,174$10,514

6 persons$137,274$11,439

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Arizona Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Arizona requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Arizona's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Arizona bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Arizona?

To file bankruptcy in Arizona, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Arizona bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Arizona?

The Chapter 7 means test in Arizona compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $65,014 per year. For a family of four, approximately $115,074. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Arizona?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Arizona. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Arizona?

Arizona has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

New Mexico · California · Nevada · Utah · Colorado

Bankruptcy Districts

$54,714

Median Income (1 person)

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Arkansas

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Arkansas Bankruptcy Districts

Eastern District of Arkansas

Western District of Arkansas

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Arkansas

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

How to File Bankruptcy in California

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 4 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in California.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in California. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

4

Bankruptcy Districts

$79,253

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in California

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct California bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

California Bankruptcy Districts

California has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Central District of California

Eastern District of California

Northern District of California

Southern District of California

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for California

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

California Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

2 persons$103,028$8,585

3 persons$120,464$10,038

4 persons$140,277$11,689

5 persons$151,377$12,614

6 persons$162,477$13,539

Bankruptcy District

$82,700

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Colorado

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Connecticut Bankruptcy Districts

Connecticut has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Connecticut

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Connecticut

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Connecticut Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$87,411$7,284

2 persons$113,634$9,469

3 persons$132,864$11,072

4 persons$154,717$12,893

5 persons$165,817$13,818

6 persons$176,917$14,743

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Connecticut Exemptions

Federal or State

Connecticut allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Connecticut's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Connecticut bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Connecticut?

To file bankruptcy in Connecticut, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Connecticut bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Connecticut?

The Chapter 7 means test in Connecticut compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $87,411 per year. For a family of four, approximately $154,717. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Connecticut?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Connecticut. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Connecticut?

Connecticut has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

New York · Massachusetts · Rhode Island

## How to File Bankruptcy in Delaware - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/delaware.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Delaware

How to File Bankruptcy in Delaware

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Delaware.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Delaware. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$72,198

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Delaware

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Delaware bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Delaware Bankruptcy Districts

Delaware has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Delaware

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Delaware

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Delaware Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$72,198$6,016

2 persons$93,857$7,821

3 persons$109,740$9,145

4 persons$127,790$10,649

5 persons$138,890$11,574

6 persons$149,990$12,499

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Delaware Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Delaware requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Delaware's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Delaware bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

To file bankruptcy in Delaware, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Delaware bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Delaware?

The Chapter 7 means test in Delaware compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $72,198 per year. For a family of four, approximately $127,790. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Delaware?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Delaware. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Delaware?

Delaware has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Pennsylvania · New Jersey · Maryland

How to File Bankruptcy in District of Columbia

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in District of Columbia.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in District of Columbia. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$99,636

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in District of Columbia

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct District of Columbia bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

District of Columbia Bankruptcy Districts

District of Columbia

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for District of Columbia

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

How to File Bankruptcy in Florida

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 3 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Florida.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Florida. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$69,876

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Florida

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Florida bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Florida Bankruptcy Districts

Florida has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of Florida

Middle District of Florida

Southern District of Florida

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Florida

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$65,628

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Georgia

## How to File Bankruptcy in Hawaii - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/hawaii.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Hawaii

How to File Bankruptcy in Hawaii

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Hawaii.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Hawaii. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$86,427

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Hawaii

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Hawaii bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Hawaii Bankruptcy Districts

Hawaii has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Hawaii

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Hawaii

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Hawaii Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$86,427$7,202

2 persons$112,355$9,362

3 persons$131,369$10,947

4 persons$152,975$12,747

5 persons$164,075$13,672

6 persons$175,175$14,597

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Hawaii Exemptions

Federal or State

Hawaii allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Hawaii's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Hawaii bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Hawaii?

To file bankruptcy in Hawaii, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Hawaii bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Hawaii?

The Chapter 7 means test in Hawaii compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $86,427 per year. For a family of four, approximately $152,975. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Hawaii?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Hawaii. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Hawaii?

Hawaii has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

California · Alaska

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project - free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

Automatic Stay ·
341 Meeting ·
Exemptions by State ·
Cost Guide ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·
Non-Dischargeable Debts

## How to File Bankruptcy in Idaho - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/idaho.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Idaho

How to File Bankruptcy in Idaho

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Idaho.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Idaho. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$65,394

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Idaho

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Idaho bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Idaho Bankruptcy Districts

Idaho has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Idaho

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Idaho

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Idaho Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$65,394$5,449

2 persons$85,012$7,084

3 persons$99,398$8,283

4 persons$115,747$9,645

5 persons$126,847$10,570

6 persons$137,947$11,495

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Idaho Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Idaho requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Idaho's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Idaho bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Idaho?

To file bankruptcy in Idaho, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Idaho bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Idaho?

The Chapter 7 means test in Idaho compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $65,394 per year. For a family of four, approximately $115,747. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Idaho?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Idaho. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Idaho?

Idaho has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

## How to File Bankruptcy in Illinois - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/illinois.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Illinois

How to File Bankruptcy in Illinois

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 3 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Illinois.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Illinois. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$74,430

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Illinois

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Illinois bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Illinois Bankruptcy Districts

Illinois has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of Illinois

Central District of Illinois

Southern District of Illinois

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Illinois

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Illinois Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$74,430$6,202

2 persons$96,759$8,063

3 persons$113,133$9,427

4 persons$131,741$10,978

5 persons$142,841$11,903

6 persons$153,941$12,828

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Illinois Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Illinois requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Illinois's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Illinois bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Illinois?

To file bankruptcy in Illinois, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Illinois bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Illinois?

The Chapter 7 means test in Illinois compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $74,430 per year. For a family of four, approximately $131,741. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Illinois?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Illinois. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Illinois?

Illinois has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

## How to File Bankruptcy in Indiana - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/indiana.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Indiana

How to File Bankruptcy in Indiana

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 2 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Indiana.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Indiana. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$63,399

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Indiana

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Indiana bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Indiana Bankruptcy Districts

Indiana has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of Indiana

Southern District of Indiana

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Indiana

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Indiana Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$63,399$5,283

2 persons$82,418$6,868

3 persons$96,366$8,030

4 persons$112,216$9,351

5 persons$123,316$10,276

6 persons$134,416$11,201

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Indiana Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Indiana requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Indiana's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Indiana bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Indiana?

To file bankruptcy in Indiana, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Indiana bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Indiana?

The Chapter 7 means test in Indiana compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $63,399 per year. For a family of four, approximately $112,216. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Indiana?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Indiana. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Indiana?

Indiana has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.
