## How to File Bankruptcy in Iowa - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/iowa.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Iowa

How to File Bankruptcy in Iowa

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 2 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Iowa.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Iowa. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$68,223

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Iowa

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Iowa bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Iowa Bankruptcy Districts

Iowa has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of Iowa

Southern District of Iowa

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Iowa

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Iowa Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$68,223$5,685

2 persons$88,689$7,390

3 persons$103,698$8,641

4 persons$120,754$10,062

5 persons$131,854$10,987

6 persons$142,954$11,912

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Iowa Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Iowa requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Iowa's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Iowa bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Iowa?

To file bankruptcy in Iowa, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Iowa bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Iowa?

The Chapter 7 means test in Iowa compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $68,223 per year. For a family of four, approximately $120,754. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Iowa?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Iowa. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Iowa?

Iowa has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Minnesota · Wisconsin · Illinois · Missouri · Nebraska · South Dakota

## How to File Bankruptcy in Kansas - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/kansas.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Kansas

How to File Bankruptcy in Kansas

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Kansas.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Kansas. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$69,197

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Kansas

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Kansas bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Kansas Bankruptcy Districts

Kansas has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Kansas

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Kansas

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Kansas Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$69,197$5,766

2 persons$89,956$7,496

3 persons$105,179$8,764

4 persons$122,478$10,206

5 persons$133,578$11,131

6 persons$144,678$12,056

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Kansas Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Kansas requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Kansas's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Kansas bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Kansas?

To file bankruptcy in Kansas, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Kansas bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Kansas?

The Chapter 7 means test in Kansas compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $69,197 per year. For a family of four, approximately $122,478. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Kansas?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Kansas. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Kansas?

Kansas has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Nebraska · Missouri · Oklahoma · Colorado

Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/kentucky.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Kentucky

How to File Bankruptcy in Kentucky

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 2 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Kentucky.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Kentucky. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$58,632

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Kentucky

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Kentucky bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Kentucky Bankruptcy Districts

Kentucky has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Eastern District of Kentucky

Western District of Kentucky

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Kentucky

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Kentucky Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$58,632$4,886

2 persons$76,221$6,351

3 persons$89,120$7,426

4 persons$103,778$8,648

5 persons$114,878$9,573

6 persons$125,978$10,498

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Kentucky Exemptions

Federal or State

Kentucky allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Kentucky's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Kentucky bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Kentucky?

To file bankruptcy in Kentucky, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Kentucky bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Kentucky?

The Chapter 7 means test in Kentucky compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $58,632 per year. For a family of four, approximately $103,778. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Kentucky?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Kentucky. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Kentucky?

Kentucky has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Louisiana

How to File Bankruptcy in Louisiana

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 3 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Louisiana.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Louisiana. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$57,528

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Louisiana

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Louisiana bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Louisiana Bankruptcy Districts

Louisiana has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Eastern District of Louisiana

Middle District of Louisiana

Western District of Louisiana

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Louisiana

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Home›States›Maine

How to File Bankruptcy in Maine

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Maine.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Maine. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$67,692

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Maine

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Step 6: 341 Meeting

5 persons$130,914$10,909

## How to File Bankruptcy in Maryland - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/maryland.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Maryland

How to File Bankruptcy in Maryland

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Maryland.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Maryland. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$97,203

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Maryland

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Maryland bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Maryland Bankruptcy Districts

Maryland has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Maryland

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Maryland

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Maryland Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$97,203$8,100

2 persons$126,363$10,530

3 persons$147,748$12,312

4 persons$172,049$14,337

5 persons$183,149$15,262

6 persons$194,249$16,187

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Maryland Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Maryland requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Maryland's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Maryland bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Maryland?

To file bankruptcy in Maryland, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Maryland bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Maryland?

The Chapter 7 means test in Maryland compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $97,203 per year. For a family of four, approximately $172,049. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Maryland?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Maryland. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Maryland?

Maryland has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Pennsylvania · Delaware · Virginia · West Virginia · District of Columbia

Filing Bankruptcy in Massachusetts - Complete Guide

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Massachusetts.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Massachusetts. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$95,727

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Massachusetts

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Massachusetts bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Massachusetts Bankruptcy Districts

Massachusetts has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Massachusetts

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Massachusetts

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Massachusetts Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$95,727$7,977

2 persons$124,445$10,370

3 persons$145,505$12,125

4 persons$169,436$14,119

5 persons$180,536$15,044

6 persons$191,636$15,969

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Massachusetts Exemptions

Federal or State

Massachusetts allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Massachusetts's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Massachusetts bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Massachusetts?

To file bankruptcy in Massachusetts, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Massachusetts bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Massachusetts?

The Chapter 7 means test in Massachusetts compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $95,727 per year. For a family of four, approximately $169,436. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Massachusetts?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Massachusetts. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Massachusetts?

Massachusetts has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

How to File Bankruptcy in Michigan

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 2 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Michigan.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Michigan. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$66,087

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Michigan

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Michigan bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Western District of Michigan

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Michigan

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Bankruptcy District

$79,161

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Minnesota

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Mississippi?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Mississippi. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Mississippi?

Mississippi has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Tennessee · Alabama · Louisiana · Arkansas

From the Open Bankruptcy Project - free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

341 Meeting ·
Exemptions by State ·
Cost Guide ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·
Non-Dischargeable Debts

## How to File Bankruptcy in Missouri - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/missouri.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Missouri

How to File Bankruptcy in Missouri

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 2 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Missouri.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Missouri. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$64,972

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Missouri

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Missouri bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Missouri Bankruptcy Districts

Missouri has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Eastern District of Missouri

Western District of Missouri

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Missouri

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Missouri Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$64,972$5,414

2 persons$84,463$7,038

3 persons$98,757$8,229

4 persons$115,000$9,583

5 persons$126,100$10,508

6 persons$137,200$11,433

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Missouri Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Missouri requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Missouri's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Missouri bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Missouri?

To file bankruptcy in Missouri, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Missouri bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Missouri?

The Chapter 7 means test in Missouri compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $64,972 per year. For a family of four, approximately $115,000. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Missouri?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Missouri. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Missouri?

Missouri has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Iowa · Illinois · Kentucky · Tennessee · Arkansas · Oklahoma · Kansas · Nebraska

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

1

Bankruptcy District

$66,501

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Montana

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Montana

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

5 persons$128,806$10,733

How to File Bankruptcy in Nebraska

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Nebraska.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Nebraska. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$71,277

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Nebraska

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Nebraska bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Nebraska

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Nevada.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Nevada. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$68,271

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Nevada

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for New Hampshire

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

New Hampshire Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$86,997$7,249

2 persons$113,096$9,424

3 persons$132,235$11,019

4 persons$153,984$12,832

5 persons$165,084$13,757

6 persons$176,184$14,682

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

New Hampshire Exemptions

Federal or State

New Hampshire allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research New Hampshire's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View New Hampshire bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in New Hampshire?

To file bankruptcy in New Hampshire, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct New Hampshire bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in New Hampshire?

The Chapter 7 means test in New Hampshire compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $86,997 per year. For a family of four, approximately $153,984. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in New Hampshire?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in New Hampshire. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within New Hampshire?

New Hampshire has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Maine · Vermont · Massachusetts

## How to File Bankruptcy in New Jersey - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/new-jersey.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›New Jersey

How to File Bankruptcy in New Jersey

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in New Jersey.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in New Jersey. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$93,051

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in New Jersey

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct New Jersey bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

New Jersey Bankruptcy Districts

New Jersey has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of New Jersey

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for New Jersey

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

New Jersey Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$93,051$7,754

2 persons$120,966$10,080

3 persons$141,437$11,786

4 persons$164,700$13,725

5 persons$175,800$14,650

6 persons$186,900$15,575

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

New Jersey Exemptions

Federal or State

New Jersey allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research New Jersey's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View New Jersey bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in New Jersey?

To file bankruptcy in New Jersey, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct New Jersey bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in New Jersey?

The Chapter 7 means test in New Jersey compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $93,051 per year. For a family of four, approximately $164,700. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in New Jersey?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in New Jersey. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within New Jersey?

New Jersey has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

New York · Pennsylvania · Delaware

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in New Mexico. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$57,528

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in New Mexico

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct New Mexico bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

New Mexico Bankruptcy Districts

New Mexico has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of New Mexico

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for New Mexico

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

New Mexico has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›New York

How to File Bankruptcy in New York

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 4 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in New York.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in New York. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

4

Bankruptcy Districts

$73,272

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in New York

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct New York bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

New York Bankruptcy Districts

New York has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of New York

Southern District of New York

Eastern District of New York

Western District of New York

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for New York

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

New York Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

## How to File Bankruptcy in North Carolina - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/north-carolina.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›North Carolina

How to File Bankruptcy in North Carolina

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 3 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in North Carolina.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in North Carolina. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$63,339

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in North Carolina

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct North Carolina bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

North Carolina Bankruptcy Districts

North Carolina has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Eastern District of North Carolina

Middle District of North Carolina

Western District of North Carolina

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for North Carolina

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

North Carolina Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$63,339$5,278

2 persons$82,340$6,861

3 persons$96,275$8,022

4 persons$112,110$9,342

5 persons$123,210$10,267

6 persons$134,310$11,192

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

North Carolina Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

North Carolina requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research North Carolina's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View North Carolina bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in North Carolina?

To file bankruptcy in North Carolina, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct North Carolina bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in North Carolina?

The Chapter 7 means test in North Carolina compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $63,339 per year. For a family of four, approximately $112,110. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in North Carolina?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in North Carolina. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within North Carolina?

North Carolina has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.
