## How to File Bankruptcy in North Dakota - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/north-dakota.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›North Dakota

How to File Bankruptcy in North Dakota

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in North Dakota.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in North Dakota. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$73,749

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in North Dakota

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct North Dakota bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

North Dakota Bankruptcy Districts

North Dakota has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of North Dakota

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for North Dakota

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

North Dakota Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,749$6,145

2 persons$95,873$7,989

3 persons$112,098$9,341

4 persons$130,535$10,877

5 persons$141,635$11,802

6 persons$152,735$12,727

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

North Dakota Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

North Dakota requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research North Dakota's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View North Dakota bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in North Dakota?

To file bankruptcy in North Dakota, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct North Dakota bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in North Dakota?

The Chapter 7 means test in North Dakota compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $73,749 per year. For a family of four, approximately $130,535. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in North Dakota?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in North Dakota. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within North Dakota?

North Dakota has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Montana · South Dakota · Minnesota

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project - free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

Automatic Stay ·
341 Meeting ·
Exemptions by State ·
Cost Guide ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·
Non-Dischargeable Debts

You May Also Find Helpful

## How to File Bankruptcy in Ohio - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/ohio.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Ohio

How to File Bankruptcy in Ohio

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 2 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Ohio.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Ohio. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$63,564

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Ohio

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Ohio bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Ohio Bankruptcy Districts

Ohio has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of Ohio

Southern District of Ohio

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Ohio

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Ohio Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$63,564$5,297

2 persons$82,633$6,886

3 persons$96,617$8,051

4 persons$112,508$9,375

5 persons$123,608$10,300

6 persons$134,708$11,225

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Ohio Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Ohio requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Ohio's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Ohio bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Ohio?

To file bankruptcy in Ohio, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Ohio bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Ohio?

The Chapter 7 means test in Ohio compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $63,564 per year. For a family of four, approximately $112,508. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Ohio?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Ohio. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Ohio?

Ohio has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

## How to File Bankruptcy in Oklahoma - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/oklahoma.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Oklahoma

How to File Bankruptcy in Oklahoma

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 3 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Oklahoma.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Oklahoma. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$60,636

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Oklahoma

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Oklahoma bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Oklahoma Bankruptcy Districts

Oklahoma has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of Oklahoma

Eastern District of Oklahoma

Western District of Oklahoma

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Oklahoma

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Oklahoma Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$60,636$5,053

2 persons$78,826$6,568

3 persons$92,166$7,680

4 persons$107,325$8,943

5 persons$118,425$9,868

6 persons$129,525$10,793

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Research Oklahoma's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Oklahoma?

To file bankruptcy in Oklahoma, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Oklahoma bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Oklahoma?

The Chapter 7 means test in Oklahoma compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $60,636 per year. For a family of four, approximately $107,325. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Oklahoma?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Oklahoma. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Oklahoma?

Oklahoma has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

## How to File Bankruptcy in Oregon - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/oregon.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Oregon

How to File Bankruptcy in Oregon

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Oregon.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Oregon. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$73,347

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Oregon

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Oregon bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Oregon Bankruptcy Districts

Oregon has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Oregon

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Oregon

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Oregon Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,347$6,112

2 persons$95,351$7,945

3 persons$111,487$9,290

4 persons$129,824$10,818

5 persons$140,924$11,743

6 persons$152,024$12,668

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Oregon Exemptions

Federal or State

Oregon allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Oregon's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Oregon bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Oregon?

To file bankruptcy in Oregon, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Oregon bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Oregon?

The Chapter 7 means test in Oregon compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $73,347 per year. For a family of four, approximately $129,824. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Oregon?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Oregon. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Oregon?

Oregon has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/pennsylvania.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Pennsylvania

How to File Bankruptcy in Pennsylvania

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 3 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Pennsylvania.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Pennsylvania. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$71,547

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Pennsylvania

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Pennsylvania bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Pennsylvania Bankruptcy Districts

Pennsylvania has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Eastern District of Pennsylvania

Middle District of Pennsylvania

Western District of Pennsylvania

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Pennsylvania

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

2 persons$93,011$7,750

3 persons$108,751$9,062

5 persons$137,738$11,478

6 persons$148,838$12,403

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Pennsylvania?

To file bankruptcy in Pennsylvania, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Pennsylvania bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Pennsylvania?

The Chapter 7 means test in Pennsylvania compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $71,547 per year. For a family of four, approximately $126,638. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Pennsylvania?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Pennsylvania. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Pennsylvania?

Pennsylvania has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

New York · New Jersey · Delaware · Maryland · West Virginia · Ohio

From the Open Bankruptcy Project - free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Ch 7 vs 13 ·

## How to File Bankruptcy in Rhode Island - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/rhode-island.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Rhode Island

How to File Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Rhode Island.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Rhode Island. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$77,358

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Rhode Island bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Districts

Rhode Island has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Rhode Island

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Rhode Island

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Rhode Island Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$77,358$6,446

2 persons$100,565$8,380

3 persons$117,584$9,798

4 persons$136,923$11,410

5 persons$148,023$12,335

6 persons$159,123$13,260

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Rhode Island Exemptions

Federal or State

Rhode Island allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Rhode Island's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Rhode Island bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

To file bankruptcy in Rhode Island, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Rhode Island bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Rhode Island?

The Chapter 7 means test in Rhode Island compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $77,358 per year. For a family of four, approximately $136,923. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Rhode Island. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Rhode Island?

Rhode Island has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Massachusetts · Connecticut

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in South Carolina.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in South Carolina. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$61,050

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in South Carolina

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct South Carolina bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

South Carolina Bankruptcy Districts

South Carolina has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of South Carolina

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for South Carolina

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

South Carolina Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$61,050$5,087

2 persons$79,365$6,613

3 persons$92,796$7,733

4 persons$108,058$9,004

5 persons$119,158$9,929

6 persons$130,258$10,854

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

South Carolina Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

South Carolina requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research South Carolina's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View South Carolina bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in South Carolina?

To file bankruptcy in South Carolina, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct South Carolina bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

The Chapter 7 means test in South Carolina compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $61,050 per year. For a family of four, approximately $108,058. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in South Carolina. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within South Carolina?

South Carolina has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Bankruptcy District

$68,208

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in South Dakota

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct South Dakota bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for South Dakota

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Bankruptcy Districts

$66,837

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Texas

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Texas bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Texas Bankruptcy Districts

Texas has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of Texas

Southern District of Texas

Eastern District of Texas

Western District of Texas

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Texas

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Texas Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$66,837$5,569

2 persons$86,888$7,240

3 persons$101,592$8,466

4 persons$118,301$9,858

5 persons$129,401$10,783

6 persons$140,501$11,708

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Texas Exemptions

Federal or State

Texas allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Texas's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Texas bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Texas?

To file bankruptcy in Texas, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Texas bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Texas?

The Chapter 7 means test in Texas compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $66,837 per year. For a family of four, approximately $118,301. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Texas?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Texas. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Texas?

Texas has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

New Mexico · Oklahoma · Arkansas · Louisiana

From the Open Bankruptcy Project - free information, no strings.

Means Test Calculator

## How to File Bankruptcy in Utah - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/utah.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Utah

How to File Bankruptcy in Utah

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Utah.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Utah. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$73,044

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Utah

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Utah bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Utah Bankruptcy Districts

Utah has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Utah

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Utah

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Utah Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,044$6,087

2 persons$94,957$7,913

3 persons$111,026$9,252

4 persons$129,287$10,773

5 persons$140,387$11,698

6 persons$151,487$12,623

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Utah Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Utah requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research Utah's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Utah bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Utah?

To file bankruptcy in Utah, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Utah bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Utah?

The Chapter 7 means test in Utah compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $73,044 per year. For a family of four, approximately $129,287. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Utah?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Utah. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Utah?

Utah has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Idaho · Wyoming · Colorado · New Mexico · Arizona · Nevada

How to File Bankruptcy in Vermont

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 1 district, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Vermont.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Vermont. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$71,007

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Vermont

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Vermont bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Vermont Bankruptcy Districts

Vermont has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

District of Vermont

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Vermont

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

2 persons$92,309$7,692

3 persons$107,930$8,994

4 persons$125,682$10,473

5 persons$136,782$11,398

6 persons$147,882$12,323

Federal or State

How do I file bankruptcy in Vermont?

You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

The Chapter 7 means test in Vermont compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $71,007 per year. For a family of four, approximately $125,682. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Vermont. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Vermont has 1 federal bankruptcy district. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

## How to File Bankruptcy in Virginia - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/virginia.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Virginia

How to File Bankruptcy in Virginia

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 2 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Virginia.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Virginia. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$84,396

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Virginia

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Virginia bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Virginia Bankruptcy Districts

Virginia has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Eastern District of Virginia

Western District of Virginia

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Virginia

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Bankruptcy Districts

$85,446

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Washington

How to File Bankruptcy in West Virginia

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in West Virginia. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$53,196

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in West Virginia

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct West Virginia bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

West Virginia Bankruptcy Districts

West Virginia has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Northern District of West Virginia

Southern District of West Virginia

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for West Virginia

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

West Virginia Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$53,196$4,433

2 persons$69,154$5,762

3 persons$80,857$6,738

4 persons$94,156$7,846

5 persons$105,256$8,771

6 persons$116,356$9,696

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

West Virginia Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

West Virginia requires filers to use state exemptions only. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available.

Research West Virginia's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View West Virginia bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in West Virginia?

To file bankruptcy in West Virginia, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct West Virginia bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in West Virginia?

The Chapter 7 means test in West Virginia compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $53,196 per year. For a family of four, approximately $94,156. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in West Virginia?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in West Virginia. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within West Virginia?

West Virginia has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.

Filing Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Pennsylvania · Ohio · Kentucky · Virginia · Maryland

## How to File Bankruptcy in Wisconsin - Step-by-Step Guide [2026]
Source: https://howtofilebankruptcy.org/states/wisconsin.html

How to File Bankruptcy | All States | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener

Home›States›Wisconsin

How to File Bankruptcy in Wisconsin

Step-by-step guide: filing process, 2 districts, fees, means test income limits, credit counseling, and what to expect in Wisconsin.

Important: This page provides educational information about filing bankruptcy in Wisconsin. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$70,092

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Step-by-Step: Filing Bankruptcy in Wisconsin

Step 1: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from a DOJ-approved provider within 180 days before filing. Online courses cost $15-$50 and take about 60-90 minutes. You will receive a certificate to file with your petition.

Step 2: Choose Your Chapter

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Your income, assets, and goals determine which chapter fits. Compare chapters.

Step 3: Gather Documents

You will need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, bank statements, a list of all debts, a list of all property and assets, monthly living expenses, and vehicle titles/registrations.

Step 4: Complete the Forms

The bankruptcy petition package includes 20+ forms (schedules A-J, statement of financial affairs, means test form). File pro se or hire an attorney. Pro se filing guide.

Step 5: File with the Court

File your petition with the correct Wisconsin bankruptcy court. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping collections, garnishments, foreclosures, and lawsuits.

Step 6: 341 Meeting

Attend the meeting of creditors (30-45 days after filing). The trustee will ask questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security card. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Step 7: Debtor Education

Complete a debtor education course (different from credit counseling) before discharge. Cost: $15-$50 online. You must file the certificate with the court.

Step 8: Receive Discharge

Chapter 7: discharge typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: discharge after completing the 3-5 year plan. The discharge eliminates your personal liability on dischargeable debts.

Wisconsin Bankruptcy Districts

Wisconsin has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Eastern District of Wisconsin

Western District of Wisconsin

Each district may have different local rules, trustee practices, and no-look fee amounts. Review local bankruptcy rules before filing.

Filing Fees

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$338 - set by the Judicial Conference (same nationwide). Can be paid in up to 4 installments over 120 days. Fee waiver available for filers below 150% of poverty line (Form 103B).

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$313 - set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments. Fee waivers are NOT available for Chapter 13, but installment plans are. Full cost breakdown.

Credit Counseling Requirement

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 109(h)) requires all bankruptcy filers to complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing. The course must be from an agency approved by the U.S. Department of Justice for your district.

Find DOJ-approved credit counseling agencies for Wisconsin

Cost: typically $15-$50 for an online course. Many agencies offer free courses for filers who cannot pay. The course takes 60-90 minutes and covers budgeting, debt management alternatives, and an assessment of your financial situation.

Wisconsin Means Test

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 and likely qualify.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$70,092$5,841

2 persons$91,119$7,593

3 persons$106,539$8,878

4 persons$124,062$10,338

5 persons$135,162$11,263

6 persons$146,262$12,188

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100. Figures effective April 2026. Source: Census/DOJ.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Wisconsin Exemptions

Federal or State

Wisconsin allows filers to choose between federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)) and state exemptions. Compare both sets to maximize your protection.

Research Wisconsin's homestead, vehicle, personal property, wildcard, and retirement exemptions before filing. Your exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7.

View Wisconsin bankruptcy exemptions in detail

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Wisconsin?

To file bankruptcy in Wisconsin, you must: (1) complete credit counseling from a DOJ-approved provider, (2) gather 6 months of income documentation, (3) fill out the official bankruptcy forms (petition, schedules, statements), (4) file with the correct Wisconsin bankruptcy court, and (5) pay the filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13). You file in the federal district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Wisconsin?

The Chapter 7 means test in Wisconsin compares your household income to the state median. For a single filer, the threshold is approximately $70,092 per year. For a family of four, approximately $124,062. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Wisconsin?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Credit counseling costs $15-$50, and the debtor education course costs another $15-$50. If you hire an attorney, expect to pay $1,000-$3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Wisconsin. You can file without an attorney (pro se) to save on legal fees.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Wisconsin?

Wisconsin has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district.
